coredata.dc:title: Carbon dot-DNA-protoporphyrin hybrid hydrogel for sustained photoinduced antimicrobial activity
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Kumari, Sonam
Rajit Prasad, Surendra
Mandal, Debabrata
Das, Prolay
coredata.dc:description: An assembly of hybrid hydrogel derived from Carbon Dot (CD), Protoporphyrin IX (PpIX) and DNA is reported here. PpIX and CD were covalently conjugated to 5′-phosphate termini of Cytosine (C) rich single strand DNA (ssDNA) separately. The CD-DNA-PpIX hybrid hydrogel was assembled through the formation of intercalated motif (i-motif) DNA network structure from CD-DNA and PpIX-DNA conjugates where CD act as crosslinkers in the hydrogel as well as energy donor to excite the photosensitizer (PS), PpIX. While hydrogel derived from only PpIX-DNA conjugate showed an inadequate sol-gel transition, the same could be precisely achieved from the CD-DNA-PpIX hybrid hydrogel by controlled pH adjustment. Distinct photophysical properties of CD and PpIX including fluorescence emission potentially enable tracking of the PS loading and hydrogel dissolution that was visually detectable under UV illumination. Reactive Oxygen Species (ROS) generation and subsequent killing of gram-positive bacteria (Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus)) were observed following excitation of PpIX (acceptor) in the hybrid hydrogel either through energy transfer from CD or by direct irradiation of PpIX with visible light. Complete dissolution of hydrogel and sustained release of PpIX and subsequent ROS generation was achieved over 10–11 days that could kill S. aureus systematically. This study provides a promising strategy to address self-quenching and solubility of PS for its sustained release in Antimicrobial Photodynamic Therapy (A-PDT) applications through smart hydrogel formulation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hydrogel
Carbon dots
Protoporphyrin
IX
i-Motif DNA
S. aureus

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Conjugation of PpIX with ethylene diamine
Conjugation of DNA with PpIX-(ED)2 and CD
In vitro PpIX release and generation of ROS
Plate count assay for bacterial viability
Photophysics of CD-DNA and PpIX-DNA conjugates and FRET confirmation
In-vitro release of PpIX from hydrogel and ROS generation
Bacterial survival by plate count assay after hydrogel treatment

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Agarose gel electrophoresis showing mobility of PpIX, PpIX-DNA, CD and CD-DNA conjugate. No staining was required in samples containing CD or PpIX, DNA (leftmost lane only) stained with Sybr Gold nucleic acid stain. (B) UV–Vis spectra of PpIX, PpIX-DNA, CD-DNA and CD-DNA-PpIX mixture. (C) Steady-state fluorescence spectra of PpIX, PpIX-DNA, CD-DNA and CD-DNA-PpIX mixture. (λex 410 nm for PpIX and PpIX-DNA, and λex 350 nm for CD-DNA and CD-DNA-PpIX).

Characterization of CD-DNA-PpIX hydrogel. (A) Circular dichroism spectra of DNA and CD-DNA-PpIX. (B) Frequency sweep test of CD-DNA-PpIX hydrogel between 0.1 and 100 rad/s and strain of 1% at 25 °C. (C) AFM image (Phase imaging mode) of CD-DNA-PpIX hydrogel. (D) SEM image of CD-DNA-PpIX hydrogel. (E) Digital images under normal light and UV light of sol and gel form of CD-DNA hydrogel (sol form), PpIX-DNA Hydrogel, and CD-DNA-PpIX hybrid hydrogel.

(A) Digital images of CD-DNA-PpIX hydrogel after incubation with supernatant PBS buffer (pH 7.4) at intervals of 24 h. (B) Steady-state fluorescence spectra of released PpIX (λexcitation = 410 nm) in the supernatant buffer collected at 24 h intervals. (C) Digital images of supernatant buffer solution collected at 24 h intervals after irradiation with UV light in the presence of DHR. (D) Steady-state fluorescence spectra of supernatant buffer collected after 24 h incubation after irradiation with UV light in presence of DHR (λexcitation = 485 nm).

Plate count assay for S. aureus. Cell survival with CD-DNA-PpIX hydrogel (PpIX:1µM) exposed to (A) UV light (B) Visible light (C) Quantitative estimation of intracellular ROS formation in S. aureus with visible light exposure (5 min) in the presence and absence of NAC. (D) Comparison of bacterial survival on exposure to UV light (2.5 min) with a recovered buffer from hydrogel on 1st, 5th, and 10th day. (E) Same as D with visible light (5 min).
Scheme for conjugation of C, rich ssDNA to CD and PpIX for hydrogel formation. The sequence of blue and red color coded DNA is the same.

head.ce:title: Carbon dot-DNA-protoporphyrin hybrid hydrogel for sustained photoinduced antimicrobial activity

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Indian Institute of Technology Patna, Patna 801103, Bihar, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Indian Institute of Technology Patna, Patna 801103, Bihar, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biotechnology, National Institute of Pharmaceutical Education and Research (NIPER), Hajipur, Bihar, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Institute of Pharmaceutical Education and Research (NIPER)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Biotechnology, National Institute of Pharmaceutical Education and Research (NIPER), Hajipur, Bihar, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An assembly of hybrid hydrogel derived from Carbon Dot (CD), Protoporphyrin IX (PpIX) and DNA is reported here. PpIX and CD were covalently conjugated to 5′-phosphate termini of Cytosine (C) rich single strand DNA (ssDNA) separately. The CD-DNA-PpIX hybrid hydrogel was assembled through the formation of intercalated motif (i-motif) DNA network structure from CD-DNA and PpIX-DNA conjugates where CD act as crosslinkers in the hydrogel as well as energy donor to excite the photosensitizer (PS), PpIX. While hydrogel derived from only PpIX-DNA conjugate showed an inadequate sol-gel transition, the same could be precisely achieved from the CD-DNA-PpIX hybrid hydrogel by controlled pH adjustment. Distinct photophysical properties of CD and PpIX including fluorescence emission potentially enable tracking of the PS loading and hydrogel dissolution that was visually detectable under UV illumination. Reactive Oxygen Species (ROS) generation and subsequent killing of gram-positive bacteria (Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus)) were observed following excitation of PpIX (acceptor) in the hybrid hydrogel either through energy transfer from CD or by direct irradiation of PpIX with visible light. Complete dissolution of hydrogel and sustained release of PpIX and subsequent ROS generation was achieved over 10–11 days that could kill S. aureus systematically. This study provides a promising strategy to address self-quenching and solubility of PS for its sustained release in Antimicrobial Photodynamic Therapy (A-PDT) applications through smart hydrogel formulation.

ce:section.ce:para: Over the past decade, DNA-based hydrogels have attained substantial interests for potential applications in biosensing, drug delivery, cell-free protein production, catalysis, and others [1–4]. Sequence-directed self-assembly, biocompatibility, biodegradability, sharp pH response, mechanical and chemical stability of DNA is translated into DNA-hydrogels, making them attractive platforms for biomedical practices [5]. Another notable property of DNA-hydrogel is their optical transparency that allows standard staining, labeling, imaging, and other photophysical studies to track drug loading and unloading for drug delivery [6,7]. Herein, we report the fabrication of a hybrid DNA-hydrogel by infusing Carbon Dot (CD) nanoparticle and a photosensitizer (PS), Protoporphyrin IX (PpIX) for the sustained release of the later to generate Reactive Oxygen Species (ROS) for potential Antibacterial Photodynamic Therapy (A-PDT) applications.

PDT has been successfully employed in certain types of cancer and has shown positives in antimicrobial therapy [8,9]. While PDT for acne treatment is a reality, A-PDT is also projected as an attractive modality for rapid wound healing and even for food packaging [10,11]. Antibacterial activity through PDT is achieved by the annihilation of bacterial cells through ROS generated from excitation of the PS upon irradiation [12]. Recently, hydrogels have been used as a matrix to hold the PS (the PDT drug) for better retention and more potent antimicrobial actions. Reportedly, supramolecular hydrogels prepared from peptide-modulated self-assembly was developed for A-PDT, as well as for antitumor PDT applications [13,14]. Particularly for A-PDT applications, hydrogels based on polymers of chitosan as well as porphyrin-based nanoparticles have been reported earlier [15,16]. However, sustained release of PDT drug from hydrogel requires pH or other physical parameter aided response that is difficult to achieve with peptide-based hydrogels, while polymer-based hydrogels for PDT applications have biocompatibility and biodegradability issues and often lack the versatility of tracking the drug cargo disbursement. Moreover, the issue of solubility and aggregation induced self-quenching of PS often undermine the efficiency of PDT irrespective of formulations. In this particular context, we believe DNA offers several advantages as a hydrogel material to host the PS and overcome the shortcomings for A-PDT applications.

Formation of DNA hydrogel from Cytosine (C) rich DNA sequences was first reported by Liu and co-workers by the creation of i-motif structures in acidic conditions [17,18]. More recently, DNA only hydrogels have been modified to increase the lattice size by incorporating gold nanoparticles, carbon nanotubes (CNT), CD and graphene as crosslinkers for network formation, effective drug loading or artificial enzymatic activity [19–23]. Elsewhere, CNT and graphene have been conjugated with PpIX, a well-known PS in aqueous suspensions for PDT, although suitable formulations need to be delineated for practical applications [24,25]. Nevertheless, aggregation and low solubility of CNT and graphene nanoparticles remain a concern for biological applicability not to mention the solubility, self-quenching and photobleaching issues of the PS. A striking alternative is the use of CD as crosslinkers in DNA-hydrogel that impart many outstanding physical and chemical features in the hydrogel. Unlike CNT and graphene, which are typically oxidized for functionalization, CDs offer numerous surface functional groups like –COOH, –NH2, and –SH for easy conjugation with DNA and other biomolecules in the aqueous phase. Additionally, notable fluorescence properties of CD provide a significant advantage to track drug cargo loading by spectroscopy and imaging studies [26–28].

For the first time, we are reporting the fabrication of a DNA-hydrogel cross-linked by covalent conjugation of DNA with fluorescent CD to accommodate PpIX as a PS for A-PDT applications. For the sustained release of PpIX and to minimize its aggregation and self-quenching, it was covalently conjugated with cytosine-rich single-stranded DNA (ssDNA) sequence that generates intermolecular i-motif based pH-responsive hydrogel by linking to ssDNA conjugated to CDs (Scheme 1 ). Apart from acting as a crosslinker, the presence of CD imparts fluorescence and expands the scope of PpIX mediated ROS generation using shorter wavelength by switching the mechanism of PpIX excitation from direct irradiation to Forster Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET). This study provides an insight to integrate fluorescence-based tracking, pH sensitivity, and resistance to PS self-quenching and photobleaching with its sustained release for controlled ROS generation within a DNA assembled hybrid hydrogel that could potentially improve A-PDT applications. Studies conducted with E. coli and S. aureus indicate selective eradication of gram-positive bacteria (S. aureus) with excellent efficiency using the hybrid hydrogel for more than one week.

Protoporphyrin IX (≥95% purity), C-rich oligonucleotide DNA (11mer, sequence 5′-PO4-CCCCTAACCCC, HPLC purified), Citric acid (≥99.5% purity), Branched Polyethylenimine (BPEI)(average Mw ∼ 800 by LS, average Mn ∼ 600 by GPC), Dihydrorhodamine 123 (DHR) (≥95% purity) and Tris Acetate-EDTA (DNAse and RNAse free) was obtained from Sigma. 1-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC.HCl) (98+% purity), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) (98% purity), ethylene diamine (ED) (98% purity), and 1-methyl imidazole (98+% purity) were obtained from Alfa Aesar. Chloromethyl 2′, 7′-dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetate (CM-H2DCFDA) (>94% purity) was obtained from Invitrogen. Luria-Bertani (LB) broth and agar, Tryptic Soya Broth (TSB) and Tryptic Soya Agar (TSA) were obtained from Himedia, India. All chemicals were used as received. Nanopure water from Millipore was used in all experiments.

Covalent conjugation of PpIX with ED was analyzed by FTIR (Perkin Elmer Spectrum 400 FTIR), 1HNMR (Bruker 400 MHz spectrometer), ESI-MS (Bruker Impact HD) as well as visualized with 1.5% agarose gel electrophoresis in 1X TAE buffer. Digital images were taken on a Canon D60 digital camera. Direct irradiation of PpIX with visible light was done using a Tungsten lamp (100 W).

UV–Visible absorption spectra were recorded on a UV-2550 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Steady-state fluorescence spectra were obtained on a Fluoromax-4 spectrofluorometer (Horiba, Japan). Time-resolved fluorescence decays were measured by a time-resolved fluorescence spectrophotometer from Edinburgh Instruments (LifeSpec-II, UK) working on time-correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) technique. The conformation of the i-motif structure of DNA was determined by Circular Dichroism spectra on a J-1500 spectropolarimeter (Jasco, UK). All measurements were performed in triplicate and in aqueous medium.

AFM image was obtained by casting a small volume of freshly prepared hydrogel on APS mica and stretching it with a glass plate. Images were captured in intermittent contact mode atomic force microscopy (ACAFM) using Agilent 5500 Scanning Probe Microscope. Commercial silicon nitride cantilevers having a force constant 40 N/m were used for the measurements (Micromesh, Bulgaria). The cantilever was oscillating at its resonance frequency ranging from 265 to 410 kHz. Frequency sweeps rheological tests of hydrogel were carried out on a modular compact rheometer (Anton Paar, MCR 302, Austria). Frequency sweep test was performed between 0.05 and 100 rad/s at 25 °C at a fixed strain of 1%. Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM) analysis was performed on a Zeiss EVO 18 microscope, the hydrogel was cast onto the coverslip at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conjugation of PpIX with ethylene diamine
ce:section.ce:para: For covalent conjugation of PpIX to ED, PpIX solution (0.2 mL, 1 mM) in DMF was activated by an aqueous solution of EDC (100 µL, 10 mM) followed by addition of aqueous NHS solution (200 μL, 10 mM). The reaction mixture was thoroughly mixed and allowed to stir at room temperature for 1 h. To this solution, ED (0.2 mL, 2 mM) was added, and a total volume reaction mixture of 1 mL was incubated for 12 h at room temperature. The reaction was quenched by adding Dichloromethane, and the organic layer was washed with water thrice and dried over anhydrous Na2SO4. The solvent was removed under vacuum to give the product (PpIX-(ED)2 as a brick red solid.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conjugation of DNA with PpIX-(ED)2 and CD
ce:section.ce:para: 5′ phosphorylated C-rich ssDNA (0.1 mL, 1 mM) was activated by addition of EDC (20 µL, 10 mM) and aqueous solution of 1-methyl imidazole (20 µL, 20 mM) at pH 6 and thoroughly mixed for 90 min at 37 °C with constant stirring. To this activated 5′-phosphorimidazolide ssDNA, 20 μL of amine functional group containing CD (5 mg/ml) was added in a total reaction volume of 200 µL with (4-(2-hydroxyethyl)-1-piperazineethanesulfonic acid) HEPES buffer (0.1 M, pH 8) and kept at 55 °C for 9 h.

Separately, a similar procedure was followed to conjugate DNA with PpIX-ED through EDC/Imidazole chemistry. Both CD-DNA conjugate and PpIX-(ED)2-(DNA)2 conjugate, designated as PpIX-DNA hereafter, was purified by dialysis (MWCO-1KDa) to remove the excess of EDC, 1-methyl imidazole and unreacted PpIX-(ED)2.

For CD-DNA hydrogel formation, purified CD-DNA conjugate was dissolved in 100 mM NaCl buffer (pH 7), heated to 90 °C for 5 min and cooled to room temperature very slowly. Similarly, for hybrid hydrogel formation, equal volumes of PpIX-DNA and CD-DNA conjugates solutions were allowed to mix in 100 mM NaCl buffer (pH 7), heated to 90 °C for 5 min and slowly cooled to room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro PpIX release and generation of ROS
ce:section.ce:para: To study the in-vitro release of PpIX from the hydrogel by erosion, the hydrogel was immersed in PBS of pH 7.4. The supernatants were collected and replaced with fresh buffer at fixed intervals of time. The release of free PpIX or PpIX-DNA from the relevant hydrogels was evaluated by measuring the steady-state fluorescence intensity of PpIX in supernatant buffer solution. Digital images of gel were also taken to display the sol-gel transition and changes in the size of the gel during the drug release process, as well as ROS generation.

Analysis of ROS generation through FRET probe CD-PpIX in hydrogel was done by adding 10 μL of DHR solution (2.5 nM) to a fixed volume of sample solutions and irradiated with a UV lamp (λex = 302 nm, 5 tubes) for 5 min at a distance of 15 cm. Oxidation of DHR 123 through ROS leads to formation of fluorescent rhodamine 123 and was measured at different time intervals by steady-state fluorescence spectroscopy and images were captured by digital camera. Estimation of ROS generation following direct irradiation of PpIX by visible light was done similarly for 5 min light exposure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Plate count assay for bacterial viability
ce:section.ce:para: Anti-bacterial activity of each hydrogel was measured by plate count assay after measuring colony forming units (CFU) of bacteria grown in agar plates. E. coli DH5α (gram negative) and S. aureus (gram-positive) cells were grown in LB broth and TSB, respectively till the OD600 value reaches ∼ 1.0 (∼109 CFU/ml). Cells were adjusted to OD600 = 0.05, and 0.1 mL (5 × 106 CFUs/per well) was added in a microtiter plate [29]. Cells were mixed with hybrid hydrogel (0.1 mM in 10 µL) in the dark and then exposed to UV light (302 nm for 2.5 min) or visible light (100 W tungsten bulb, 300–900 nm, for 5 min). The hydrogel-untreated cells but either exposed or unexposed to UV/visible light were used as a control. Cells were incubated for 12 and 24 h and then serially diluted in sterile PBS (pH 7.4), and 0.1 mL of diluted cells were plated in LB agar and TSA plates for E. coli and S. aureus cells, respectively in triplicates [30]. Agar plates were further incubated for 24 h at 37 ℃. CFUs counted from hydrogel –untreated cells were considered 100% and reduction in CFUs for hydrogel-treated cells were presented. Error bars were generated by calculating standard deviation from the average of three data points and statistical significance between samples were evaluated by performing unpaired t-tests. Data were considered statistically significant and highly significant when P < 0.05 and P < 0.001, respectively (*P < 0.05; **0.05 < P < 0.01; ***0.01 < P < 0.001).

S. aureus cells were treated with hybrid/hydrogel and exposed to UV/visible light as described above. Treated cells were pre-incubated with ROS scavenger N-acetyl l-cysteine (NAC, 1 mM) when required. Cells were incubated for 3 h, and then intracellular ROS was measured by incubating with CM-H2DCFDA (20 µM) at 37 ℃ for 30 min in the dark. Acetate groups of CM-H2DCFDA, a chloromethyl derivative of H2DCFDA, are cleaved by intracellular esterases, and its thiol-reactive chloromethyl group reacts with intracellular thiols and subsequent oxidation yields a fluorescent product. Fluorescence was measured at an excitation/ emission wavelength of 492/530 nm by Micro Plate Reader (BioTeK, Synergy/H1).

PpIX was first conjugated to ED to create pendant amine functionality for subsequent attachment to 5′-phosphate terminated ssDNA. The conjugation was done by reacting PpIX with an excess of ED after EDC activation of its carboxylic acid groups to achieve the corresponding di-amide PpIX-(ED)2 (Scheme 1). Covalent conjugation of PpIX and ED was confirmed by FTIR, 1H NMR, and HRMS. The FTIR spectrum of PpIX-(ED)2 shows peaks at 1399 cm−1 and 1646 cm−1 corresponding to CO and CN stretching that confirms the formation of an amide bond [31]. The broad peak at 3446 cm−1 due to NH stretching also endorses the presence of NH2 in PpIX-(ED)2 (Fig. S1, SI). 1H NMR spectra of PpIX-(ED)2 conjugate ascertain the presence of protons corresponding to amide (10.09 ppm), amine (8.21 ppm) and methylene (6.17 and 4.12 ppm) groups (Fig. S2, SI). The absence of proton in 1H NMR from the carboxylic acid group of PpIX (δ ∼ 12.3 ppm) further confirms the formation of PpIX and ED covalent conjugate. Moreover, molecular weight determination by ESI-Mass analysis showed the molecular mass of PpIX-(ED)2 conjugate [calculated mass (M + 1) = 647.3816, observed mass = 647.1668] (Fig. S3 SI).

Purified PpIX-(ED)2 was further conjugated with 5′-phosphate terminated C rich ssDNA by EDC and Methyl-Imidazole mediated phosphoramidite coupling chemistry to obtain PpIX-(ED)2-(DNA)2, represented as PpIX-DNA herein. In agarose gel electrophoresis, the mobility of PpIX-DNA was less compared to only PpIX as well as free DNA that hint towards the conjugation of PpIX-(ED)2 with DNA (Fig. 1 A). Circular dichroism spectra of DNA exhibit a positive band at ∼276 nm and a negative band at ∼245 nm confirming the right-handed helicity of DNA which was maintained after its conjugation with PpIX-(ED)2 in alkaline condition (pH 8) (fig. S4, SI) [32].

The aqueous soluble CD with surface amine functionality was synthesized following previously reported method by low-temperature pyrolysis of citric acid and BPEI as precursors and characterized [33,34]. The CD exhibited graphitic morphology as evident from powder XRD experiments (Fig. S5, SI) with size ∼5 nm diameter, which was confirmed from TEM images (S6, SI). The presence of pendant amine functional groups on the surface of the CD was determined by FT-IR that showed a broad peak at 3484 cm−1 (Fig. S7, SI). The amine groups on the CD were covalently conjugated with 5′-phosphorylated C-rich DNA sequence through EDC and Me-Imidazole mediated coupling. The unreacted salts, DNA or CD residues were eliminated by dialysis. Electrophoretic gel mobility of CD was retarded after conjugation with DNA, which provides evidence of conjugation of CD with DNA (Fig. 1A).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photophysics of CD-DNA and PpIX-DNA conjugates and FRET confirmation
ce:section.ce:para: The absorbance maxima peak at 450 nm of PpIX remains unchanged after its conjugation with C-rich DNA with the emergence of a faint shoulder at ∼270 nm due to the presence of DNA as determined by UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy (Fig. 1B). The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of CD-DNA conjugate also showed one shoulder at ∼270 nm due to the presence of DNA along with an absorption peak at 350 nm due to CD (Fig. 1B).

Steady-state fluorescence spectra of PpIX-DNA, as well as CD-DNA conjugate, did not show any significant changes in fluorescence emission intensity and shift in emission wavelength upon conjugation of PpIX and CD respectively with DNA (Fig. 1C and S8A, SI). Efficient overlap of emission spectra of CD with absorption spectra of PpIX was observed that confers FRET between CD (donor) and Pp IX (acceptor). The intensity of CD emission decreased significantly in CD-DNA-PpIX assembly, indicating energy transfer from CD to PpIX by FRET mechanism. The occurrence of FRET was further confirmed by exciting CD and measuring its fluorescence lifetime in CD-DNA and CD-DNA mixed with PpIX-DNA samples. A substantial decrease in the lifetime of CD was observed for CD-DNA and PpIX-DNA mixture in slightly basic medium (Fig. S8B, SI).

C rich DNA sequence show pH-induced protonation at Cs that create H-bonds to crosslink the DNA strands into i-motif structures leading to the formation of the hydrogel in acidic pH (∼pH 5.5) [18]. In the present study, the DNA strands were conjugated to CD and PpIX separately to generate hybrid hydrogels that displayed sol-gel transition at neutral pH. This is not uncommon since i-motif structure have been observed previously at neutral pH depending on the DNA sequence, molecular crowding, and other environmental conditions [35,36]. At high pH, C units remained isolated in random coils because of their electrostatic repulsion. When the pH drops below 7, the Cs become partially protonated, which initiate the formation of i-motif structures. The presence of PpIX and CD in the hybrid hydrogel prevent the formation of random coils at around neutral pH, and Hemi-protonation of C was achieved easily at pH 7. Likewise, we have fabricated three different hydrogels to study the sustained release of ROS upon PpIX irradiation from the hydrogels. A hydrogel was produced from a solution containing CD-DNA conjugate in 100 mM NaCl buffer by slowly decreasing the pH from 8 to 7. A hybrid hydrogel CD-DNA-PpIX was constructed by adjusting the pH of a mixture of an equal amount of CD-DNA and PpIX-DNA conjugates in the same buffer that showed similar sol-gel transition as CD-DNA hydrogel. Hydrogel resulting from only PpIX-DNA was also obtained by similar pH adjustment. However, the sol-gel transition of PpIX-DNA hydrogel was found to be inconsistent and did not show a sharp transition with pH change. For this reason, subsequent studies for ROS generation and cell killing were not performed with this PpIX-DNA hydrogel.

The emergence of i-motif structure induced crosslinking in the hydrogels was analyzed by circular dichroism spectra of the samples. At higher pH (∼8), DNA in free form or conjugated to CD and PpIX showed a positive band at ∼276 nm and a negative band at ∼244 nm. However, upon slowly reducing the pH to 7, a negative band at ∼290 nm and a positive band at ∼262 nm were obtained for all the three hydrogels, which indicate the formation of intermolecular i-motif structure in those samples (Fig. 2 A and Fig. S4, SI) [37]. Evidently, the formation of i-motif structure points towards the alignment of CDs in close proximity to PpIX at fixed distances defined by the length of the DNA in the CD-DNA-PpIX hybrid network. This assembly also ensures that PpIX self-quenching by aggregation is significantly inhibited for improved efficacy of ROS generation after excitation.

The mechanical property of the DNA hydrogels was studied by the rheological test. The hydrogels were analyzed by frequency sweep to determine the linear equilibrium modulus. The frequency dependence of shear storage (G′) and shear loss (G″) moduli are characteristic analytical properties of hydrogels. A material can behave like a viscoelastic gel when G′ > G″ at the high-frequency region and like liquid when G″ > G′ at low-frequency region. The shear-storage modulus shows a higher value than shear-loss modulus over the frequency range of 0.05–100 rad/s which confirms the gel-like structure of the hydrogels over the entire frequency sweep (Fig. 2B) [38] For CD-DNA and CD-DNA-PpIX samples at pH 7, the G′ was found to increase with an increase in the frequency that is signature of the gel-like structure. However, at a higher frequency, G′ and G″ intersect for PpIX-DNA hydrogel with an increase in loss modulus than storage modulus that indicates a breakdown of the gel and hence its non-sustenance. The reduced mechanical strength of only PpIX-DNA hydrogel is attributed to the lack of crosslinking since PpIX have two attachment point with DNA (Scheme 1).

AFM image shows the presence of CD in a well-scattered form in the CD-DNA-PpIX hydrogel, where Inter-CD distance was found to vary from ∼10–16 nm (Fig. 2C). It suggests that the DNA strands in the hydrogel display some stretching and contraction in the three-dimensional network. SEM images of CD-DNA-PpIX show that the DNA-self assembly holds well to give a rugged yet porous surface to the hydrogel, where CDs are found to be embedded in the matrix.

Digital images obtained after hydrogel formation clearly indicate loss of fluidity of CD-DNA-PpIX hybrid hydrogels at pH 7 (Fig. 2E). The CD-DNA hydrogel appears yellowish in daylight while intense blue fluorescence was observed when viewed under UV light. PpIX-DNA and CD-DNA-PpIX hydrogels appear light brownish in daylight while red fluorescence was observed from them when viewed with UV light due to the presence of PpIX. Similar optical behavior was noted for CD-DNA hydrogel that was co-incubated with free PpIX. Thus, the loading of PpIX, either free or conjugated with DNA in the hydrogels, could be visually ascertained by observing the change in fluorescence emission. The ROS generated by the PS during PDT not only cause the death of tumor cells or microbes but also cause degradation of the PS on prolonging irradiation. Tracking of PS is important in PDT that serves as a therapy efficiency indicator and point towards optimization of dosage and frequency of administration of the PS to achieve an optimal therapeutic effect. The nano-assembly generated herein provide an opportunity to track the PS loading and release, which is desirable in PDT.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In-vitro release of PpIX from hydrogel and ROS generation
ce:section.ce:para: The CD-DNA (infused with free PpIX) and CD-DNA-PpIX hydrogels were incubated with a fixed volume of PBS buffer, pH 7.4 (physiological pH) at 37 °C for 24 h. The supernatant buffer was collected after 24 h and investigated for the presence of PpIX (free or conjugated with DNA) by analyzing the fluorescence emission of PpIX. Further, the supernatant was irradiated with UV light or visible light for 5 min in the presence of non-fluorescent DHR (to be converted into fluorescent Rhodamine after reaction with ROS), and fluorescence of the solution was measured. This gives a quantitative measure of ROS generation upon excitation of PpIX that has leached out of the hydrogel. These operations were repeated every 24 h until the hydrogel get completely solubilized in the buffer. Digital images of hydrogels were taken during PpIX release study, which showed a gradual decrease in the size of gel resulting from its degradation with time (Fig. 3 A).

Sustained release of PpIX was observed for the CD-DNA-PpIX hybrid hydrogel for more than a week (Fig. 3B). Both digital images and fluorescence spectra of the supernatant buffer in the presence of DHR after UV irradiation showed ROS generation over ten days that confirmed FRET from CD to PpIX through excitation of the former by UV light where PpIX does not absorb (Fig. 3C and D). However, a gradual decrease in ROS generation was observed with passing days where ROS generated on the 10th day was one third compared to the first day.

Generation of ROS was also observed when the supernatant PBS buffer was irradiated with visible light (Fig. S9, SI). For 5 min irradiation, the magnitude of ROS generation was found to be little higher with visible light (direct irradiation of PpIX) than UV irradiation (CD-PpIX FRET) (Fig. S10, SI). CD-DNA hydrogel in which free PpIX was incorporated, showed random and burst release of PpIX amounting to ∼90% release from the hydrogel in 4 days only (Fig. S11). Self-quenching of neutral Porphyrins due to aggregation is known to undermine the effect of ROS generation upon excitation [39]. While aggregation induced self-quenching of PpIX was minimized in the hybrid hydrogel by conjugating PpIX with DNA and crosslinking with CD-DNA, such effect evidently gained significance and reduce ROS generation efficiency with time for CD-DNA hydrogel where PpIX was accommodated in the free state. The release of PpIX from DNA-only hydrogel (pH 5) was also abrupt and could not be studied at physiological pH where it exists in sol form. Due to inconsistent sol-gel transition and poor hydrogel properties of PpIX-DNA conjugate, ROS generation study was not performed with this sample too.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bacterial survival by plate count assay after hydrogel treatment
ce:section.ce:para: PDT using porphyrin has been found to be effective in inactivation of bacterial and yeast biofilms and also against Candida albicans [29,40]. The efficacy of A-PDT has shown promise in a mouse model with infected resistant strains of S. aureus [41]. During the photochemical mechanism of PDT, light activated PS react with ground state oxygen to produce superoxide radical followed by hydroxyl radicals (•OH) and singlet oxygen (1O2) in two different pathways [42]. The ROS comprising of superoxide anion, hydrogen peroxide, •OH and 1O2 are extremely toxic, and lethal for cells since there is no enzyme which can detoxify •OH and 1O2 [43]. Therefore, both •OH and 1O2 are highly reactive that can damage nearly all types of biomolecules (proteins, lipids, and nucleic acids) and induce bactericidal effect [44,45]. Here, we have tested the efficacy of PpIX-based hybrid hydrogel against S. aureus and E. coli after photoactivation followed by incubation for 12 and 24 h to see the bactericidal effect of produced ROS. UV irradiation for 2.5 min followed by incubation for 24 h affected >4.5 log (>99.99%) reduction of S. aureus cells for CD-DNA-PpIX hydrogel (10 µM) and >2.8 log (>99.8%) reduction for CD hydrogel mixed with free PpIX (Fig. 4 A). This effect is dose-dependent since 2.5 µM of CD-DNA-PpIX hydrogel, and CD hydrogel mixed with free PpIX showed >1.4 log (>95%) and > 1 log (>90%) reduction in cell survival, respectively (Fig S11, SI) under similar conditions. Thus, CD-DNA-PpIX hybrid hydrogel and CD hydrogel mixed with free PpIX showed significant antibacterial activity when irradiated by UV light. However, CD hydrogel and PpIX alone did not show any noteworthy killing of S. aureus cells after UV exposure (Fig. 4A). This is obvious due to the fact that the CD used herein is unable to produce any ROS upon irradiation and PpIX is only excited at a higher wavelength (>400 nm). Here, FRET between and CD and PpIX is responsible for the excitation of PpIX and subsequent production of ROS where CD acts as a donor. This also points towards the expedited killing of bacteria with a very short exposure of UV irradiation using the hybrid hydrogel.

The hybrid hydrogel was also exposed to visible light to observe cell killing where PpIX was directly excited to produce ROS. Irradiation with visible light for 5 min caused >4.8 log reduction (>99.99%) in cell numbers for CD-DNA-PpIX hydrogel and >2.5 log (>99.5%) reduction for CD hydrogel mixed with free PpIX (Fig. 4B) compared to hydrogel-untreated cells. While CD hydrogel devoid of any PpIX did not show any cell killing, a solution of free PpIX (similar concentration as in hydrogels) showed >1.2 log (>93%) reduction in cell viability with visible light. Our experiments clearly showed that greater efficiency of cell killing was achieved when the hydrogel was involved. Self-quenching of PpIX leads to less killing of cells, which is observed in CD hydrogel mixed with PpIX and more prominently in free PpIX. This result undoubtedly points towards the advantages of covalent linking of PpIX with DNA for increasing the efficiency of ROS generation.

Measurement of ROS in hybrid hydrogel-treated and visible-light exposed cells showed a similar trend as observed in S. aureus killing assay. We observed ∼2.8, ∼2.3 and ∼1.7-fold increase in ROS production (which can be abrogated by pre-incubation with ROS inhibitor NAC) for CD-DNA-PpIX hydrogel, CD hydrogel mixed with PpIX and free PpIX-treated cells after visible light exposure (Fig. 4C). After UV exposure, we observed ∼2.1 and ∼2.4-fold increase in ROS production for CD-DNA-PpIX hydrogel and CD hydrogel mixed with PpIX, respectively, but not for free PpIX (Fig S12, SI).

Both CD-DNA-PpIX hydrogel and CD hydrogel mixed with free PpIX showed a decreasing trend in killing efficacy with an increasing day of extraction when S. aureus cells were exposed to UV or visible light. S. aureus cells were killed with 1st, 5th and 10th day released CD-DNA-PpIX hydrogel samples (collected buffer) after UV irradiation with >5.5 log, >2.7 log and >1.7 log reduction in cell numbers, respectively (Fig. 4D). With released CD hydrogel mixed with PpIX, >4.4 log, >1.5 log and >1.2 log killing of S. aureus cells were observed under similar conditions.

On visible light irradiation, >5 log, ∼2.3 log and >1.1 log killing of S. aureus cells with 1st, 5th and 10th day released CD-DNA-PpIX hybrid hydrogel samples were observed (Fig. 4E). With released free PpIX (obtained from CD-DNA hydrogel), >4 log killing was observed with 1st day released samples with decreasing trend in killing efficacy of 5th and 10th day-released samples. Thus, the cell killing was more than double with CD-DNA-PpIX hybrid when compared to CD-hydrogel mixed with PpIX after 10 days of hydrogel extraction. Overall, with hybrid hydrogel, an enhanced sustained release was found to occur, leading to better cell killing in a span of 10 days. No significant killing of gram-negative E. coli cells in the presence of hydrogel or PpIX after UV or visible light irradiation were found as the membrane of gram-negative bacteria is impermeable to neutral porphyrins including PpIX (Fig. S13) [46,47].

In summary, PpIX-ED and amine functionalized CD was covalently conjugated to C-rich ssDNA to construct a pH-responsive hybrid DNA hydrogel CD-DNA-PpIX by mixing PpIX-DNA and CD-DNA covalent conjugates. PpIX (acceptor) was excited either through UV-irradiation of CD (FRET donor) or directly with visible light that results in ROS generation and provides a provision to tune the wavelength of excitation for ROS generation per requirement. The intense fluorescence of PpIX was retained in the hydrogel that may be potentially tracked to correlate the efficacy of its sustained release and ROS generation. The in-vitro release of PpIX from the hybrid hydrogel resulting from its disruption was observed at physiological pH through a span of 10 days with a gradual decrease in ROS formation with time. However, PpIX loosely accommodated in CD-DNA hydrogel was released more quickly that points towards the importance and necessity of creating PpIX-DNA conjugate and engage it in fabricating the hybrid hydrogel for sustained release. Hydrogel assembled from only PpIX-DNA conjugate lack sharp sol-gel transition and found to be devoid of physical characteristics and robustness typical of a hydrogel, mainly due to reduced DNA crosslinking and the presence of an increased amount of hydrophobic PpIX. The release of PpIX from this hydrogel was no better than free PpIX that further justify the use of crosslinking CD-DNA units in hydrogel fabrication. Selective killing of gram-positive bacteria through ROS was found to be highly efficient and persistent when hybrid CD-DNA-PpIX hydrogel was used. Overall, our study has demonstrated that sustained release of photosensitizer and simultaneous controlled ROS generation could be achieved through CD nanoparticle, a photosensitizer (PpIX) and DNA assembly in a hybrid hydrogel formulation that presents a biocompatible delivery platform for antibacterial or other relevant photodynamic therapy applications. Moreover, the aggregation and self-quenching of photosensitizer could be effectively minimized following our strategy of covalent conjugation with DNA.

A novel pH-responsive hybrid hydrogel was constructed from Carbon Dot (CD)-DNA and Protoporphyrin IX (PpIX)-DNA covalent conjugates through i-motif induced self-assembly of the DNA strands. PpIX was found to generate Reactive Oxygen Species (ROS) upon excitation either by direct irradiation with visible light or through Forster Radius Energy Transfer (FRET) from CD (donor). The magnitude of ROS generated from PpIX that is conjugated to DNA in the CD-DNA-PpIX hydrogel matrix was found to be significantly higher than from free PpIX. The hybrid hydrogel was found to release PpIX in a sustained manner for more than 10 days and offer much better antibacterial activity.

The major aim of this study was to develop a methodology and formulation that minimize or possibly eliminate aggregation induced self-quenching and solubility issues of well-known photosensitizers (PS) like PpIX and guarantee its sustained release for improved efficacy in typical Antimicrobial Photodynamic Therapy (A-PDT) applications. We hypothesized the creation of a DNA-hydrogel where Protoporphyrin IX (PpIX) would be covalently linked to the Cytosine rich DNA to address the above issues. However, we did not observe noteworthy hydrogel properties and sustainability of the PpIX release from the hydrogel that prompted us to tune our methodology to include Carbon Dot (CD) as a crosslinker in the DNA hydrogel to disperse the PpIX. In addition to its role as a crosslinker in the formulation of the hybrid hydrogel, the CD was also endowed with an additional role of an effective FRET donor to excite PpIX. Thus, ROS generation could be achieved through PpIX excitation by a dual mechanism, by FRET through UV irradiation of CD (donor) and also by direct irradiation of PpIX with visible light. This has certain advantages in PDT applications, mainly resistance to photobleaching of the PS and widening the window of irradiation wavelength [48]. The distance between CD and PpIX in the hydrogel could be precisely controlled by modifying the DNA sequence length for tuning FRET efficiency and abating self-quenching of PpIX. Under no circumstances, aggregation of free PpIX is possible in the hybrid hydrogel that ensures much improved ROS generation in a sustained manner. Sustained release also guarantees the protection of the PS cargo from degradation and enhance photostability, maintain an optimum dosage for a long time to avoid repeat administration, and improve biodistribution.

Previously, hydrogels from mostly polymer sources have been developed that act as a matrix to hold the PDT drug and inhibit aggregation. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first example where a hybrid DNA-hydrogel has been used to host the PDT drug for inhibition of aggregation induced self-quenching and sustained release simultaneously. Earlier, PpIX has been conjugated with DNA to create an antimicrobial nanonetwork, but the system was devoid of any hydrogel properties [49]. The current system offers a definite formulation in the form of a hydrogel for practical usage and should gain importance where supreme biocompatibility of the matrix and sustained release of Photosensitizer (PS) are desired. Since the hydrogel is DNA based, we foresee its application in antitumor PDT also. The presence of CD and hydrogel matrix offers immense opportunity to hold relevant drug molecules through various non-covalent interactions that essentially point towards an emerging prospect of combination anticancer Chemotherapy-PDT.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors acknowledge IIT Patna and NIPER Hajipur for infrastructure and research facilities and SAIF IIT Patna for HRMS facility. SK is thankful to Dept. of Biotechnology for fellowship.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2019.06.034.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
